data.title: Garmin (GRMN) Stock Looks Fully Valued After A 141% Run
data.description:
Garmin stock has delivered a strong 141.0% return over the past three years. However, the current valuation picture looks more balanced, with the intrinsic value estimate from a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model and market multiples both pointing to a share price that now appears closer to fair than clearly cheap.

  • Over the past three years Garmin has returned 141.0%, which puts recent pullbacks into context and raises the question of how much of the story is already reflected in the price.
  • Recent product and platform moves, such as acquisitions in training software and new aviation and wearable offerings, can support expectations for future cash flows. At the same time, execution risks around integrating these additions and maintaining returns on invested capital may limit how much investors are willing to pay.
  • Garmin scores 2 out of 6 on broader valuation checks, which suggests the stock leans expensive rather than a clear bargain, even though the DCF based intrinsic value points to it being fairly valued overall. You can see that score detail at 2/6.

The issue now is whether Garmin's current price already reflects the quality of the business and recent developments, or if there is still a margin of safety left in the valuation.

Garmin delivered 5.3% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Consumer Durables industry.

Advertisement

Where Does Garmin Sit on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) method estimates what Garmin might be worth today based on projected future cash generation. For Garmin, the latest twelve-month free cash flow sits at about $1.49b, with the model assuming that cash flows grow rather than shrink over time before settling into a steadier phase.

Based on those assumptions, the DCF output points to an intrinsic value of about $254 per share, which is close to the current market price and implies only a small 5.4% discount. Because the recent acquisitions of TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic expand Garmin's fitness ecosystem, the market may already be factoring in a reasonable portion of the potential benefits into the current share price.

Overall, the DCF workup suggests Garmin stock is roughly fairly valued at current levels.

Garmin is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Garmin.

Where Does Garmin Sit on Earnings?

The P/E multiple is a useful cross check for Garmin because earnings are a key driver of how investors typically value established hardware and software businesses.

Garmin trades on a P/E of about 26.7x, which is roughly double the wider Consumer Durables average of 13.0x and sits below the peer group average of 31.9x. On this framework, a tailored fair P/E of about 25.1x is implied, based on factors such as Garmin's margins, scale and risk profile. This places the current multiple slightly above that reference point but not by a wide margin.

That small gap suggests the market is pricing Garmin broadly in line with what its earnings profile would support, rather than treating the stock as either a clear bargain or stretched outlier.

Overall, Garmin appears roughly fairly valued on its P/E multiple.

These narratives are available on the company’s Community page. Each one treats its view of fair value as a thesis about Garmin's business that you can revisit over time, rather than a static snapshot.

Community views on Garmin sit on a wide spectrum, with some investors focusing on product momentum and others flagging competitive and regulatory pressure.

data.title: Apple (AAPL) Faces Samsung’s New Foldables As Foldable iPhone Expectations Build
data.description:
• Samsung has launched three new foldable smartphones, adding fresh competition in the premium device segment.
  • The launches come as Apple, ticker NasdaqGS:AAPL, is reported to be preparing its own foldable device entry.
  • The timing sets up a direct comparison between Samsung's current foldable line up and expectations for a potential foldable iPhone.

For investors watching Apple, the latest move from Samsung focuses attention on how a future foldable iPhone could fit into Apple's broader product line. Apple shares recently closed at $321.66, with the stock up 18.7% year to date and 51.1% over the past year. Returns of 68.8% over three years and 126.6% over five years show how closely the market tends to track shifts in Apple's hardware cycle.

The expectation that Apple may capture significant share of the foldable smartphone segment adds weight to how and when the company enters this category. Samsung's new devices effectively set a benchmark for pricing, features, and user experience that Apple will be measured against. For shareholders, the key question is how a foldable product might influence Apple's future device mix and longer term revenue profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Apple by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Apple.

Does the team leading Apple have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

For Apple, the timing of Samsung’s three new foldable phones puts John Ternus’ early product decisions under the spotlight. Ternus has been closely linked to Apple’s hardware roadmap, so investors will be watching how a future foldable iPhone reflects his approach to balancing design, durability, and integration with Apple Intelligence and services. Samsung, Huawei and other Android handset makers already have multiple foldable generations in market, so Apple’s leadership team is effectively choosing to arrive later with a product that will be judged against a defined set of features, form factors and price points.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Apple Narrative

  • The expected foldable iPhone supports the existing Apple narrative that new device formats, paired with AI-powered features and a growing services ecosystem, can keep users inside the Apple hardware and software loop for longer.
  • Samsung’s head start in foldables, together with strong efforts from Huawei and Xiaomi, challenges the assumption that Apple can rely solely on ecosystem strength without matching or exceeding competitors’ hardware experiments.
  • The narrative around Apple focuses heavily on AI assistants, services and emerging markets, but it does not fully address how leadership under John Ternus might handle higher hardware complexity, repairability and long-term support for foldable devices.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Apple to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A misstep on foldable hardware under new leadership, whether through reliability issues or a design that fails to stand out against Samsung and Huawei, could raise questions about Apple’s product-execution track record.
  • ⚠️ Entering the foldable category at premium pricing without clear differentiation could compress unit volumes or push customers toward cheaper Android alternatives, especially if repair costs or durability concerns surface.
  • 🎁 A carefully timed foldable launch that integrates tightly with Apple Intelligence, services and wearables could reinforce Apple’s ecosystem and give Ternus early credibility as a product-focused CEO.
  • 🎁 If Apple uses its late entry to address pain points seen in early foldables, such as hinge reliability and screen longevity, it could strengthen perceptions of quality and help sustain high-end iPhone demand.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how Apple under John Ternus talks about foldables on earnings calls and product events, especially any comments on where a foldable iPhone would sit in the existing line up. Pay attention to how quickly Samsung iterates on its new Galaxy Z Fold and Z Flip models, and whether Apple references those devices when framing its own choices. It is also worth tracking investor and user reaction to early leaks or prototypes, as this will offer clues on whether Apple is prioritizing ultra-premium pricing, mass-market appeal, or a gradual, lower-volume introduction into the foldable category.

Nokia Oyj is widely associated with network infrastructure and 5G equipment, and this new work with NestAI shows the company applying its technology in defense settings. Deployable 5G networks with AI driven command and control sit at the intersection of secure communications and complex field operations, an area that many telecom peers have not focused on. For investors, this sits next to Q2 2026 results that showed €4,815 million in quarterly sales and net income of €2 million, as well as guidance that assumes a 3% to 7% quarter-on-quarter net sales increase for Q3 2026. data.title: CSX (CSX) Stock Faces Questions As Q2 EPS Growth Lags Bullish Narratives
data.description:
CSX (CSX) has just posted Q2 2026 revenue of US$3,935 million with basic EPS of US$0.54, alongside trailing 12 month revenue of US$14.5 billion and EPS of US$1.73 that frame the latest quarter in a wider context. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$3,574 million and EPS of US$0.44 in Q2 2025 to US$3,935 million and EPS of US$0.54 in Q2 2026. Trailing 12 month revenue has ranged between US$14.1 billion and US$14.5 billion, and EPS between US$1.54 and US$1.73 over the past year. With net profit margins holding in the low 20s, this set of results gives investors a clean look at how CSX is converting its revenue base into consistent profitability.

See our full analysis for CSX.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how CSX's latest results line up with the most common narratives around its growth, profitability, and risk profile, and where those stories might need updating.

See what the community is saying about CSX

Advertisement

TTM revenue and margins hold steady around US$14.5b and 22.2%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, CSX has reported about US$14.5b in revenue and US$3.2b in net income, which translates to a 22.2% net profit margin compared with 21.9% a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative expects margin expansion over time, and the current 22.2% margin provides a concrete reference point against that view, as:
    • Analysts are assuming margins could move toward around 26.2% in a few years, so the present level shows some gap between today and that medium term target.
    • The modest change from 21.9% to 22.2% aligns with the idea of gradual, rather than rapid, efficiency gains that are still consistent with the consensus outlook.

CSX earnings growth at 3.9% trails forecast 8.9%

  • Over the last year, CSX's earnings grew 3.9% while the supplied data notes earnings are forecast to grow about 8.9% per year, highlighting a gap between recent performance and expectations.
  • Bulls point to volume opportunities and cost programs, and the current numbers partly support that optimism but also set some guardrails:
    • Bullish assumptions look for revenue growth of around 7% annually and a move in margins from roughly the low 20s toward the high 20s, whereas the latest trailing figures show revenue growth of 4.6% and margin at 22.2%.
    • This combination supports the bullish case that CSX is already profitable with room to improve, while reminding investors that the step up from 3.9% actual earnings growth to 8.9% forecast growth still needs to be earned through execution on projects like industrial development and intermodal expansion.

Bulls argue CSX's cost discipline and project pipeline could make the recent 3.9% earnings growth a starting point rather than the ceiling, and you can see how that argument stacks up in the 🐂 CSX Bull Case

Valuation wedged between P/E of 30.4x and DCF fair value of US$46.57

  • CSX trades on a P/E of 30.4x, slightly above the 30.1x peer average but below the broader US Transportation industry at 37.9x, while the current share price of US$52.81 sits above the DCF fair value of about US$46.57.
  • Critics highlight debt and pricing as key concerns, and the data offers a mixed read for that more cautious, bearish angle:
    • The high level of debt is flagged as a minor risk, which lines up with bears who worry that capital intensive projects and leverage could limit flexibility even if earnings grow.
    • At the same time, a P/E that is lower than the wider Transportation group but higher than peers, along with a price above DCF fair value, reinforces the bearish claim that investors should think carefully about how much of the 8.9% forecast earnings growth and margin improvement is already reflected in the current valuation.

Skeptics warn that paying above a DCF fair value of US$46.57 with a P/E of 30.4x only makes sense if CSX hits those mid to high single digit earnings growth targets, and you can see how that cautious view is built in the 🐻 CSX Bear Case

data.title: Core Lithium Stock And 2 Tariff Exempt Picks Worth Watching
data.description:
New U.S. tariffs of 10% and 12.5% on imports from major partners like the EU and China keep trade tensions in focus, even as many key products such as oil, gas and major food items remain exempt. For investors, that mix of pressure and protection can shift costs, pricing power and sentiment in uneven ways across the market. This article looks at three stocks whose businesses appear closely tied to these import exemptions and trade terms. It aims to help you think through where tariff exposure might be lower, where it might still matter and how that could influence each stock’s risk profile.

Advertisement

Core Lithium (ASX:CXO)

Overview: Core Lithium is an Australian resources company focused on developing lithium projects and other metal deposits in the Northern Territory and South Australia, targeting minerals such as gold, silver, uranium, lead, zinc, rare earths and base metals.

Market Cap: A$825.4m

Core Lithium sits at the intersection of two themes that matter for U.S. Import-Exempted Industries right now: critical minerals supply and tariff exemptions. As an Australian lithium developer with projects that can supply exempt critical minerals into the U.S., it operates under relatively stable trade terms while many competitors face higher import costs. The company carries meaningful risks, including current losses, past shareholder dilution and execution uncertainty around restarting operations and improving processing efficiency. It also holds cash with no debt, has drilled into additional lithium and gold potential, and plans to spin out non-core exploration into Axiant Resources, which could sharpen its focus. Investors considering this stock may wish to pay particular attention to these trade, financial and operational dynamics when conducting their own research.

Core Lithium’s stalled operations, clean balance sheet and tariff friendly positioning could be masking a sharper turning point than the market appreciates. It may be worth reading the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs (2 are major!)

Avino Silver & Gold Mines (TSX:ASM)

Overview: Avino Silver & Gold Mines is a Vancouver based precious metals company that acquires, explores and advances silver, gold, copper and base metal projects in Mexico, anchored by its 100% owned Avino Mine complex in Durango.

Operations: Avino Silver & Gold Mines generates US$112.8m in revenue from metals and mining activities focused on gold and other precious metals, all originating from operations in Mexico.

Market Cap: CA$1.50b

Avino Silver & Gold Mines stands out in the U.S. Import Exempted Industries context because its silver and critical mineral output can move into the U.S. under tariff friendly terms, supporting pricing power when many peers face higher import costs. The business combines forecast double digit earnings and revenue growth with a 32.7% net margin, but the P/E of 26.1x, high reliance on external borrowing, dilution and recent insider selling mean investors are paying for that growth with added risk. Recent results show both strong year on year earnings and some pressure on production volumes, so anyone looking at the stock may want to understand how Avino plans to balance capital spending, debt and production scale under this new tariff regime.

Avino Silver & Gold Mines looks like growth at a price, with solid margins and tariff friendly access to the U.S. that many miss. Before you decide what the P/E of 26.1x is really signaling, read the 3 key rewards and 3 important warning signs (1 is major!)

TAT Technologies (TATT)

Overview: TAT Technologies provides maintenance, repair and overhaul services and manufactures heat transfer systems and other components for commercial and military aircraft, supporting airlines, cargo carriers and defense customers worldwide.

Operations: TAT Technologies generates most of its revenue from MRO Services for Aviation Components at US$83.8m, followed by MRO Services for Heat Transfer Components and OEM of Heat Transfer Solutions at US$43.1m, OEM of Heat Transfer Solutions and Aviation Accessories at US$42.1m, and Overhaul and Coating of Jet Engine Components at US$9.5m, with a small offset from inter company eliminations of US$1.4m.

Market Cap: US$538.2m

TAT Technologies sits in a favorable position under the new U.S. tariff regime, because aircraft parts are exempt and the company already generates substantial revenue from the United States. As a result, its MRO and heat transfer businesses avoid extra import costs that could affect less protected suppliers. A record backlog of about US$580m, fresh multi year MRO contracts and an exclusive global distribution deal for key Honeywell APU platforms provide revenue visibility and support an ongoing margin improvement story. At the same time, reliance on cyclical MRO demand, supply chain constraints and a balance sheet funded entirely with external borrowing mean the growth case involves meaningful risks, which is one reason some investors are watching TAT Technologies closely at present.

TAT Technologies looks like an overlooked aircraft supplier with a record US$580m backlog and fresh multi year deals that could reshape its earnings profile. Before assuming the story is straightforward, scan the analyst forecasts for TAT Technologies

data.title: Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR) Just Reported Second-Quarter Earnings: Have Analysts Changed Their Mind On The Stock?
data.description:
Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 4.9% to US$68.85 in the week after its latest second-quarter results. It looks like the results were a bit of a negative overall. While revenues of US$175m were in line with analyst predictions, statutory earnings were less than expected, missing estimates by 2.4% to hit US$1.43 per share. The analysts typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. We've gathered the most recent statutory forecasts to see whether the analysts have changed their earnings models, following these results.

Taking into account the latest results, the most recent consensus for Banner from four analysts is for revenues of US$734.0m in 2026. If met, it would imply a meaningful 9.3% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory per-share earnings are expected to be US$6.04, roughly flat on the last 12 months. In the lead-up to this report, the analysts had been modelling revenues of US$722.7m and earnings per share (EPS) of US$6.17 in 2026. The analysts seem to have become a little more negative on the business after the latest results, given the minor downgrade to their earnings per share numbers for next year.

View our latest analysis for Banner

The consensus price target held steady at US$73.67, with the analysts seemingly voting that their lower forecast earnings are not expected to lead to a lower stock price in the foreseeable future. Fixating on a single price target can be unwise though, since the consensus target is effectively the average of analyst price targets. As a result, some investors like to look at the range of estimates to see if there are any diverging opinions on the company's valuation. There are some variant perceptions on Banner, with the most bullish analyst valuing it at US$77.00 and the most bearish at US$70.00 per share. The narrow spread of estimates could suggest that the business' future is relatively easy to value, or thatthe analysts have a strong view on its prospects.

Of course, another way to look at these forecasts is to place them into context against the industry itself. The analysts are definitely expecting Banner's growth to accelerate, with the forecast 19% annualised growth to the end of 2026 ranking favourably alongside historical growth of 1.1% per annum over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 7.6% per year. It seems obvious that, while the growth outlook is brighter than the recent past, the analysts also expect Banner to grow faster than the wider industry.

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analysts reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for Banner. Fortunately, they also reconfirmed their revenue numbers, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. Additionally, our data suggests that revenue is expected to grow faster than the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. At Simply Wall St, we have a full range of analyst estimates for Banner going out to 2027, and you can see them free on our platform here..
